agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Order Service directly couples to Courier Service's database, creating a critical architectural violation with race conditions and hidden schema dependencies."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Cross-service direct database access violates service boundary",
      "risk": "Order Service holds a direct connection pool to Courier Service's database, creating tight coupling that defeats the purpose of service separation. Any schema change in courier_db (column rename, table restructure, status enum change) silently breaks the Order Service with no compile-time or contract-level protection. Courier Service team cannot evolve their schema independently.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "order.service.ts:4-9 (courierDb Pool) and line 13 (direct query)",
      "reproduction": "1. Courier team renames `status` column to `availability_status` or changes zone model. 2. Order Service query fails at runtime with no prior warning. 3. All order assignments break in production.",
      "fix": "Replace direct DB access with a call to a Courier Service API (e.g., `GET /couriers/available?zone=X`) or consume courier availability via domain events. The Courier Service owns its data and exposes it through a versioned contract.",
      "time_to_fix": "4-8 hours (API endpoint + client integration)",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service isolation, independent deployability, courier assignment reliability"
    },
    {
      "title": "Race condition on courier assignment — no locking or reservation",
      "risk": "Multiple concurrent Order Service instances (or even concurrent calls within one instance) can read the same available courier simultaneously. Both see courier X as 'available', both assign courier X to different orders. The courier's status is never updated by this code — it only updates the order record. Result: double-booking couriers, one order silently has a courier who is actually serving another order.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "order.service.ts:13-21 (read-then-assign without atomicity)",
      "reproduction": "1. Two orders arrive simultaneously for the same zone. 2. Both queries return the same courier as rows[0]. 3. Both orders update their own record with that courier's ID. 4. Courier is double-booked; no error is raised.",
      "fix": "The Courier Service should expose a reserve/claim endpoint (e.g., `POST /couriers/reserve`) that atomically selects and marks a courier as reserved using SELECT FOR UPDATE or an atomic UPDATE ... RETURNING within a transaction. The Order Service should call this endpoint instead of reading raw data.",
      "time_to_fix": "2-4 hours (atomic reservation endpoint in Courier Service)",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Courier assignment correctness, no double-booking"
    },
    {
      "title": "Courier status never updated — ghost availability",
      "risk": "After assigning a courier to an order, the code updates the order record but never sets the courier's status to 'assigned' or 'busy'. The courier remains 'available' in the courier_db, so subsequent queries will keep returning the same courier for new orders indefinitely. Even if the race condition were fixed, this is a data consistency bug.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "order.service.ts:20 (only updates orders table, not couriers table)",
      "reproduction": "1. Assign courier to order A. 2. Courier still shows status='available' in courier_db. 3. Next order also gets same courier. 4. Repeat forever.",
      "fix": "Courier status must be updated atomically as part of the assignment. With the recommended API approach, the Courier Service's reserve endpoint handles this internally within a single transaction.",
      "time_to_fix": "1 hour (as part of the API redesign above)",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Courier availability accuracy, assignment correctness"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "Leaked database credentials cross service boundary",
      "description": "Order Service requires COURIER_DB_USER and COURIER_DB_PASSWORD environment variables, meaning courier database credentials must be distributed to the order service's deployment. This expands the blast radius of a credential leak and violates least-privilege — the Order Service can read (and potentially write) any table in courier_db.",
      "location": "order.service.ts:5-8"
    },
    {
      "title": "No connection pool limits or error handling on cross-service DB pool",
      "description": "The courierDb Pool has no max connections, connectionTimeoutMillis, or idleTimeoutMillis configured. Under load, the Order Service could exhaust connections on the Courier database, degrading or taking down the Courier Service itself.",
      "location": "order.service.ts:4-9"
    },
    {
      "title": "Naive courier selection (always rows[0])",
      "description": "Always picking the first row with no ORDER BY clause means courier selection depends on arbitrary database row order. No load balancing, no fairness, no proximity optimization. One courier will be hammered while others sit idle.",
      "location": "order.service.ts:19"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Introduce a Courier Service API with a POST /couriers/reserve endpoint that atomically claims a courier, returning the assigned courier ID or a 409 if none available.",
    "Consider an event-driven model where couriers publish availability changes and the Order Service maintains a local read model, reducing synchronous coupling.",
    "Add circuit breaker logic around the courier assignment call so Order Service degrades gracefully when the Courier Service is unavailable."
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Order Service cannot deploy independently of Courier Service database schema. Any courier_db migration requires coordinated deployment with Order Service, defeating microservice independence.",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback of Order Service is safe in isolation, but any courier_db schema changes made alongside this code cannot be rolled back independently.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Current code has no API contract — it reads raw tables. There is no backward compatibility surface to protect.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No monitoring on the cross-service DB connection. Connection pool exhaustion, query failures, and double-bookings will be silent.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": true,
      "details": [
        "Cross-service DB credentials leaked into Order Service config",
        "Courier assignment has no atomicity guarantees — data integrity risk"
      ]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert Order Service deployment. No courier_db changes are made by this code so courier side needs no rollback. However, any orders assigned during the window may have double-booked couriers requiring manual reconciliation."
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "architectural_concern",
      "description": "Order Service directly queries Courier Service's database, bypassing service boundaries entirely. The fix is not a code patch — it requires designing a new API contract between Order and Courier services (or an event-driven availability model). This is a fundamental service communication pattern decision.",
      "routed_to": "MJ",
      "blocked_criteria": [
        "Courier assignment correctness",
        "Service isolation and independent deployability",
        "No double-booking of couriers"
      ],
      "recommendation": "MJ should design the inter-service contract: synchronous API (POST /couriers/reserve with atomic claim semantics) vs event-driven (courier availability events with local projection). Shaq implements after MJ's design is approved."
    }
  ],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 72,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Cross-service DB coupling is unambiguously an architectural violation",
      "Race condition on courier selection is confirmed by read-then-write pattern with no locking",
      "Missing courier status update is visible in the code — only orders table is written"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether there is other code not shown that updates courier status elsewhere",
      "Whether the system is currently single-instance (which would reduce but not eliminate race risk)",
      "Whether a Courier API already exists and this code is a legacy bypass"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "This is the complete courier assignment flow — no other code updates courier status",
      "Multiple instances of Order Service may run concurrently in production",
      "Courier Service is intended to be an independent service with its own data ownership"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me classify this and produce my review.

This is a textbook cross-service database coupling issue. The Order Service is directly connecting to the Courier Service's database. This is an architectural concern — the fix requires changing how services communicate.

Classification: `architectural_concern` — Service A reads Service B's database directly, needs API/events instead.

Let me produce the output.

This is a fundamental service communication pattern decision.",
      "routed_to": "MJ",

      "Cross-service DB coupling is unambiguously an architectural violation",
